coredata.dc:title: Impact of red wine polyphenol carbon dots on the oxidative stability of oils
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Minxian
Huang, Guohuan
Li, Yuanlin
Huang, Yongguang
coredata.dc:description: Red wine polyphenols carbon dots (P-CDs) with antioxidant properties were synthesized and extensively characterized to determine their size, morphology, and microstructure. Their potential to enhance oxidative stability in sunflower oil was thoroughly evaluated and compared with traditional antioxidants like butyl hydroxyanisole (BHA) and n-propyl gallate (PG). The P-CDs were found to be spherical nanoparticles, generally smaller than 10 nm, confirming their nanoscale characteristics. Further analysis revealed that the unique properties of the P-CDs were largely due to oxygen-containing functional groups and a highly defective graphite structure, providing them with distinct physical and chemical attributes. In scavenging tests with DPPH and ABTS radicals, P-CDs demonstrated outstanding performance, suggesting their promising application in the antioxidant field. Additionally, the P-CDs effectively inhibited the oxidative rancidity process in sunflower oil, offering a novel approach to improving oil's oxidative stability. A significant finding was that when 0.01% of red wine polyphenol carbon dots were added, they outperformed both BHA and PG in inhibiting the accumulation of anisidine, reducing total polar compound content, and preserving the oil's fatty acid composition, color, and viscosity. This discovery not only broadens the application scope for P-CDs in antioxidant research related to oils, but also supports their practical implementation in the food industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Free radicals
Antioxidant activity
Red wine polyphenols
Carbon dots
Oil oxidation
Physical and chemical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of antioxidant activity of P-CDs
Preparation of samples of oils and fats with added biomass P-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 32060534 and 32360571), Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (Natural Science) (Grant No. ZK [2023] 118, 166), Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology Support Project (Grant No. [2023] 343), Guizhou Provincial Department of Information Industry Project (Grant No. [2020]198), Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Guizhou Province (Qianke Zhongyindi [2019]4019), General horizontal project (Grant No. K22-0115-014, Qianrenlingfa [2020]8), Guizhou University Natural Science Special Scientific Research Fund Project (X2021327), Talents Introduction Projects of Guizhou University (Grant No. [2020]5) and Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (Natural Science) (Grant No. ZK[2024] Major Project 016).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of P-CDs, (b) particle size distribution of P-CDs, (c) FTIR spectra of P-CDs, and (d) XRD patterns of P-CDs.
(a) UV–visible absorption spectrum of P-CDs, (b) XPS survey pattern of P-CDs, and (c–d) High-resolution C 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of P-CDs.

(a) Absorption spectra of DPPH solutions at different P-CDs concentrations, (b) Scavenging of DPPH free radicals by P-CDs (inset: variation of DPPH solutions with P-CDs content), (c) Absorption spectra of ABTS radicals solutions at different P-CDs concentrations, (d) Scavenging of ABTS radicals by P-CDs (inset: variation of ABTS radicals solutions with P-CDs content).
Changes in (a) AV, (b) PV, and (c) p-AnV in oils with different antioxidants heated for 5 h.
Cumulative changes in fatty acids after heating at 170 °C for 5 h a) Control b) Oil samples with 0.01% P-CDs added c) Oil samples with 0.01% BHA added d) Oil samples with 0.01% PG added.
Viscosity change of sunflower oil treated with different antioxidants during 5 h heating process.
Color changes of heating oils treated with different antioxidants.

head.ce:title: Impact of red wine polyphenol carbon dots on the oxidative stability of oils

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering and Biological Pharmacy of Guizhou Province, College of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang, Guizhou, 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering and Biological Pharmacy of Guizhou Province
College of Liquor and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering and Biological Pharmacy of Guizhou Province, College of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang, Guizhou, 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi Minzu University, Nanning, 530006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi Minzu University, Nanning, 530006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red wine polyphenols carbon dots (P-CDs) with antioxidant properties were synthesized and extensively characterized to determine their size, morphology, and microstructure. Their potential to enhance oxidative stability in sunflower oil was thoroughly evaluated and compared with traditional antioxidants like butyl hydroxyanisole (BHA) and n-propyl gallate (PG). The P-CDs were found to be spherical nanoparticles, generally smaller than 10 nm, confirming their nanoscale characteristics. Further analysis revealed that the unique properties of the P-CDs were largely due to oxygen-containing functional groups and a highly defective graphite structure, providing them with distinct physical and chemical attributes. In scavenging tests with DPPH and ABTS radicals, P-CDs demonstrated outstanding performance, suggesting their promising application in the antioxidant field. Additionally, the P-CDs effectively inhibited the oxidative rancidity process in sunflower oil, offering a novel approach to improving oil's oxidative stability. A significant finding was that when 0.01% of red wine polyphenol carbon dots were added, they outperformed both BHA and PG in inhibiting the accumulation of anisidine, reducing total polar compound content, and preserving the oil's fatty acid composition, color, and viscosity. This discovery not only broadens the application scope for P-CDs in antioxidant research related to oils, but also supports their practical implementation in the food industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Deep-frying as a cooking and food-drying technique involves immersing food in hot oil, allowing for rapid cooking through efficient heat transfer (Dehghannya & Ngadi, 2021). According to the preliminary reference list of carcinogens released by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) of the World Health Organization (WHO) on October 27, 2017, deep-frying and the high-temperature emissions it produces have been categorized as a Group 2A potential carcinogen. Several factors, including temperature, light, oxygen, moisture, and metal ions, play key roles in the complex chemical reactions of deep-frying, contributing to the oxidation of oils. This oxidation process is harmful, generating a range of hazardous substances that pose potential health risks to humans (Rodríguez et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020). When oils are exposed to high temperatures, they are highly susceptible to oxidation, resulting in the formation of compounds such as polymers, carbonyls, and epoxy groups. These compounds not only alter the original properties of the oils but can also be detrimental to human health (Ganesan & Xu, 2020; Grootveld et al., 2020). Therefore, it is crucial to thoroughly investigate antioxidant treatments for oils used in high-temperature cooking to ensure food safety and promote human health.

Antioxidants play a crucial role in preventing the oxidation of fats and can be broadly categorized into two groups: synthetic and natural antioxidants. Common synthetic antioxidants include terbutylhydroquinone (TBHQ), butylhydroxyanisole (BHA), dibutylhydroxytoluene (BHT), propyl gallate (PG), and ascorbyl palmitate (AP). Natural antioxidants, derived from natural sources, include vitamin E, tea polyphenols, bamboo leaf antioxidants, and rosemary extract, all of which have inherent antioxidant properties (Bharti & Singh, 2020; Flieger et al., 2021). While synthetic antioxidants offer benefits in terms of production efficiency, stability, and versatility, studies indicate that their potential toxicity and carcinogenicity are causes for concern. Additionally, the manufacturing of synthetic antioxidants involves raw materials and chemical processes that can generate waste, leading to environmental issues such as air and water pollution. These environmental concerns not only disrupt ecosystems but also indirectly increase the risk of human exposure to harmful substances (Parcheta et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2021). Due to these risks, researchers have increasingly focused on finding alternatives to synthetic antioxidants. They are exploring safer and more eco-friendly natural antioxidants to combat lipid oxidation while mitigating health and environmental risks.

Carbon dots are a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with significant fluorescence properties (Li et al., 2021). The hydrothermal method is currently the preferred technique for synthesizing carbon dots because it's versatile, easy to use, and more energy-efficient than other methods (Meng et al., 2022). When choosing materials for carbon dot preparation, natural products are often selected due to their numerous benefits. Their environmental friendliness, simplicity of preparation, atom economy, high biocompatibility, and nutrient content make them ideal raw materials for carbon dot synthesis. Sugars like glucose, sucrose, and fructose, as well as biomass such as wood, straw, shells, and other agricultural and forestry wastes, along with fresh or spoiled fruits and vegetables, are excellent sources for creating carbon dots (Atchudan et al., 2020; Perumal et al., 2021). These materials are not only widely available and easily accessible, but after hydrothermal treatment, the resulting carbon dots can exhibit unique luminescent properties, opening up new possibilities for their applications.

The use of carbon dots in food processing and preservation brings together the dual benefits of their fluorescent properties and biocompatibility (Luo et al., 2020). As an emerging fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots exhibit excellent fluorescence and stability, making them an ideal tool for rapid food detection and traceability (Zhu et al., 2021). By constructing fluorescent sensors based on carbon dots, hazardous substances, additives, and nutrients in food can be quickly and accurately detected, helping to ensure food safety and quality (Fan et al., 2020). Additionally, the fluorescent properties of carbon dots can be used in food packaging to create intelligent systems that help prevent counterfeiting, thereby increasing the technology content and added value of packaging materials (Fu et al., 2022). Carbon dots also hold significant potential in food preservation due to their high biocompatibility and low toxicity. Integrating carbon dots into food packaging materials allows the creation of smart packaging with antioxidant and antimicrobial properties, which helps extend food freshness and reduce spoilage (Li et al., 2022). Biomass serves as a precursor for the production of carbon dots and offers a wide and diverse range of sources, providing flexibility in manufacturing. Carbon dots derived from biomass are not only biocompatible but also possess antioxidant properties. For example, certain phenolic natural products can be used as precursors to create carbon dots that effectively scavenge reactive oxygen species and demonstrate strong antioxidant activity (Wang et al., 2021). Despite the advancements in carbon dots for antioxidation, the mechanism behind their antioxidant effects is still complex and not fully understood. The antioxidant performance of carbon dots can vary based on factors like preparation method, structure, and surface properties. Consequently, further research is needed to clarify the antioxidant mechanisms and optimize the performance of carbon dots, laying a strong foundation for their broader use in food processing and preservation.

Scientists have discovered that red wine extracts offer significant health benefits. Red wine contains polyphenols, such as gallic acid, catechins, quercetin, proanthocyanidins, and resveratrol, all of which have strong antioxidant properties—sometimes even more potent than Vitamin E (Nemzer et al., 2022). Red wine polyphenols, also known as red grape polyphenols, are key compounds extracted from red wine (Gutierrez-Escobar et al., 2021). These polyphenols primarily come from the skins and seeds of wine grapes, and they are extracted into the grape juice through the maceration process during winemaking. Red wine polyphenols display a wide range of biologically significant properties, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antiviral, cosmetic, and immunostimulatory effects. They are also known to possess various other beneficial biological activities (Buljeta et al., 2023). It's noteworthy that red wine polyphenols are considered the most potent antioxidants in red wine extracts due to their high stoichiometric ratio properties. However, the impact of carbon dots derived from red wine polyphenols (P-CDs) on the consistency and quality of frying oils has not been widely studied. Thus, a thorough examination of the antioxidative mechanisms of carbon dots made from red wine polyphenols and their influence on the stability and quality of frying oils is crucial in food science research. This study not only advances our understanding of the biological activities of red wine polyphenol carbon dots, but it could also provide new preservation methods and quality control techniques for the food industry.

This study begins with a comprehensive analysis of the key characteristics of P-CDs, focusing on their shape, size, and molecular composition in relation to their antioxidant properties. Next, the effectiveness of P-CDs, BHA, and PG in enhancing the oxidative stability and overall quality of frying oils was thoroughly evaluated. The results were then carefully compared with control oil samples that contained no antioxidant additives, providing a detailed comparison to understand the mechanisms and effects of P-CDs and other antioxidants in maintaining the quality of frying oils. Through this study, a more scientific and effective approach to oil quality control can be developed for the food industry, encouraging broader use of natural antioxidants like P-CDs in food preservation.

Red wine and sunflower oil were acquired from a nearby supermarket located in Guiyang, China. All additional reagents were chromatographically pure or analytically pure and were procured from Adamas, located in Shanghai, China.

The extraction of red wine polyphenols used Ricci's method (Scano, 2021). To begin, 1 g of red wine polyphenols was placed in a hydrothermal reaction vessel with 25 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min, then heated in a muffle furnace at 150 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the material was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm (4440 g) for 10 min (293.15 K) to separate the solids. The supernatant was placed in a 500-Da dialysis bag and dialyzed for up to 24 h to remove small molecular impurities. Finally, the dialyzed liquid was freeze-dried to obtain pure carbon dots (CDs) powder (Zhao et al., 2021).

The morphological characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (model: JEOL JEM-2100, Tokyo, Japan) at a high voltage of 200 kV. To determine the particle size distribution of the CDs, a particle size analyzer (model: Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Worcestershire, UK) was used. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained with an infrared spectrometer (model: Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS10, Waltham, MA, USA) to identify the chemical structure of the CDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) (model: D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany) can be used to analyze its crystal structure and lattice parameters. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (model: AXIS SUPRA+, Shimadzu, Japan) is used to study the chemical composition, chemical state, and electronic structure of materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of antioxidant activity of P-CDs

ce:section.ce:para:
To assess the capacity of P-CDs to neutralize DPPH radicals, this experiment followed the method outlined below (Khoshkalampour et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2023). First, a 2 mL ethanolic DPPH solution with a concentration of 0.1 mM was prepared. This solution was mixed with 2 mL of vitamin C solution at different concentrations (0–20 μg/mL) and with 2 mL of P-CDs solution at various concentrations (0–128 μg/mL). The mixtures were then allowed to react for 20 min under light-protected conditions to ensure accurate results. After the reaction was complete, the absorbance of each mixture was measured at 517 nm using a spectrophotometer, and the results were recorded. To calculate the ability of P-CDs to neutralize DPPH radicals, the following formula was used:
(1)
%
D P P H
s c a v e n g i n g
c a p a c i t y =
(
1 −
A i
A 0
)
× 100 %
where A₀ signifies the absorption of DPPH solution blended with a blank solvent, and A₁ indicates the absorption of DPPH solution blended with various concentrations of P-CDs. Using this equation, the elimination activity of the P-CDs towards DPPH radicals at different concentrations can be precisely calculated, thereby enabling the evaluation of their antioxidant capabilities. The DPPH scavenging capacity was quantified as IC50, which was determined in triplicate for consistency.

To evaluate the ability of P-CDs to scavenge ABTS radicals, this study used previously established methods (Abdel-Hamid et al., 2019; Du et al., 2022; Han et al., 2023), with some modifications. A 7 mmol/L ABTS stock solution was prepared and set aside. A 140 mmol/L potassium persulfate solution was also prepared. Next, 10 mL of the ABTS stock solution was mixed with 176 μL of the potassium persulfate solution and thoroughly shaken. The ABTS radicals stock solution was then generated by allowing the mixture to sit in the dark at room temperature (293.15 K) for 12–16 h. This stock solution was diluted with absolute ethanol until the absorbance at 734 nm was approximately 0.900, then set aside. Following this, 4 mL of the ABTS working solution was mixed with 1 mL of vitamin C solution at varying concentrations (0–4.5 μg/mL), and 1 mL of P-CDs solution at different concentrations (0–4.8 μg/mL), ensuring the total volume was 5 mL. The mixture was vortexed at room temperature (293.15 K) and kept in the dark for 30 min to ensure complete reaction. After this period, a spectrophotometer was used to measure the absorbance of the reaction solution at 734 nm. The scavenging ability of P-CDs for ABTS radicals was calculated using the following formula:
(2)
% ABTS
scavenging
capacity =
(
1 −
A j
A 0
)
× 100 %
where A₀ signifies the absorption capacity of ABTS working solution combined with a blank solvent, and A₁ represents the absorption capacity of ABTS working solution mixed with varying concentrations of P-CDs. Using this method, the scavenging effect of the P-CDs on ABTS radicals at different concentrations was quantitatively evaluated to gain insight into their antioxidant properties. Vitamin C is a benchmark antioxidant. The scavenging ability of ABTS radicals was expressed as IC50 and was determined three times in parallel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of samples of oils and fats with added biomass P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: This study aimed to evaluate the impact of adding P-CDs, BHA, and PG at a concentration of 0.01% on the oxidation levels during the thermal oxidation of sunflower oil. Each antioxidant was mixed with 100 mL of sunflower oil, and then ultrasonicated for 20 min to ensure uniform distribution of P-CDs, BHA, and PG in the oil. Afterward, the oil samples containing these antioxidants were placed in an oil bath at 170 °C for a continuous 5 h period. During this process, 20 mL samples were taken every 1 h, immediately cooled to room temperature, and later analyzed for specific physicochemical indices, including peroxide value and acid value, which reflect the degree of oxidation during the heating process. After 5 h, the heating process was stopped. Additionally, gas chromatography was used to measure the oxidized products. This technique allows for the separation and detection of volatile compounds in oils and fats, providing insights into the impact of P-CDs on oxidation during the thermal oxidation of sunflower oil. For effective comparison, a control group of oil without any added antioxidants was subjected to the same heating and sampling procedures. By comparing and analyzing the data from the experimental group and the control group, the antioxidant properties of P-CDs and their effectiveness in reducing oxidation during the thermal processing of sunflower oil could be accurately assessed.

The acid value (AV), peroxide value (PV), and p-anisidine value (p-AnV) of the oil samples were measured according to the national standards of the People's Republic of China: GB 5009.229–2016 for AV (GB 5009.229-2016, 2016), GB 5009.227–2023 for PV (GB 5009.227-2023, 2023), and GB/T 24304–2009 for p-AnV (GB/T 24304-2009, 2009).

A gas chromatograph (Foley, GC9720 Plus, Zhejiang, China) equipped with a flame ionization detector (FID) and a FFAP column (30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm) was used to measure fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs). Boron trifluoride was used to methylate the fatty acids according to the national standard GB 5009.168–2016 (GB 5009.168-2016, 2016).

To accurately assess the color characteristics of oil samples in line with the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) standard, a colorimeter (model: Konica Minolta CR-400, Tokyo, Japan) was used. This instrument measured three parameters - L*, a*, and b* - to determine the brightness level (L*), the red-green tone (a*), and the yellow-blue color characteristics (b*) of the oil samples.

A turntable viscometer with a rotor (model: Lichen NDJ-8S, Shaoxing, China) was used to measure the viscosity of the oil sample under controlled conditions, with a fixed volume of 200 mL and a rotation speed of 60 rpm. The goal was to acquire precise data on the oil sample's viscosity to accurately evaluate its flow properties.

The experimental design followed the principle of complete randomization to ensure the objectivity and repeatability of the results. The entire experiment was repeated three times to reduce the risk of random errors. Additionally, each oil sample was measured three times to increase data stability and reliability. For data analysis, a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to thoroughly process the collected data. To further validate the results from the ANOVA, a Tukey test was performed at a 95% confidence level, allowing for the identification of statistically significant differences between the means of different groups. This approach increases the reliability and robustness of the experimental conclusions. For statistical analysis, the study used SPSS software (version 21.0), developed by SPSS Inc., based in Chicago, Illinois, USA.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Fig. 1 a shows spherical nanoparticles with excellent monodispersity in P-CDs derived from red wine polyphenols. The particle size distribution data in Fig. 1b indicates that the diameter of the P-CDs ranges from 0.0 to 3.5 nm, with most particles being larger than 0.5 nm (Özcan et al., 2020).

The molecular structure of P-CDs was initially examined using Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra, as shown in Fig. 1c. The strong absorption peak at 3400 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the O–H bond. The absorbance peaks at 1705 cm−1 and 1641 cm−1 correspond to the stretch-bending vibrations of CO and C–C, respectively, while the peak at 1035 cm−1 represents the C–O stretching vibration (Ding et al., 2016; Wongrerkdee & Pimpang, 2020). These findings suggest that the P-CDs contain oxygen functional groups, including carbonyl and hydroxyl groups, as well as a significant amount of CC bonds. The X-ray Diffraction (XRD) results presented in Fig. 1d show a distinct diffraction peak at 21.66°, which is consistent with the interlayer spacing characteristic of graphite structures (Salar Khaledian et al., 2021).

The UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 2 a) of the P-CDs solution shows two major absorption peaks at 215 nm and 275 nm, corresponding to the π → π* transition of aromatic C–C and the n → σ* transition of a conjugated system, respectively (Torrisi et al., 2024). In the X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Fig. 2b), C–C/CC (284.6 eV), C–O (285.8 eV), and CO (286.3 eV) were present in the C 1 s spectrum of P-CDs (Fig. 2c), and the O1s spectrum contained CO (531.5 eV) and C–O (533.1 eV) (Fig. 2d) (Saravanan et al., 2021). These findings are consistent with the earlier FTIR analysis, indicating that the surface of the P-CDs is rich in various functional groups (see Fig. 1c).

Based on the experimental results, the IC50 value, which quantifies antioxidant activity, was used to measure the ability of P-CDs to neutralize DPPH radicals and ABTS radicals. P-CDs showed similar effectiveness in scavenging free radicals compared to vitamin C. The IC50 values of vitamin C against DPPH radicals and ABTS radicals, which were used as reference points, were 4.81 μg/mL and 3.21 μg/mL, respectively.

As the concentration of P-CDs increased, the absorbance of DPPH radicals at specific wavelengths decreased significantly, indicating a dose-dependent reduction of DPPH radicals by P-CDs. The scavenging activity increased markedly from 10.50% to 90.62% as the concentration of P-CDs rose from 1 μg/mL to 20 μg/mL. Moreover, in Fig. 3a and 3b , as DPPH radicals were neutralized, the color of the ethanol-based DPPH solution changed from dark purple to yellow, due to the formation of the stabilizing molecule DPPH-H (Shen et al., 2017). The IC50 value for P-CDs against DPPH radicals was 5.21 μg/mL, demonstrating a high scavenging ability, although slightly higher than vitamin C's 4.81 μg/mL. The impressive scavenging capacity of P-CDs is attributed to their diverse array of surface functional groups, especially hydroxyl groups, which act as key hydrogen donors in neutralizing free radicals.

In the ABTS radicals scavenging assay, P-CDs also performed exceptionally well. As the concentration of P-CDs increased from 0.8 μg/mL to 4.8 μg/mL, their scavenging activity rose significantly from 20.68% to 76.04%. Additionally, the dark green ABTS solution gradually lightened as it was treated with P-CDs, confirming their effectiveness in scavenging ABTS radicals. Notably, in Fig. 3c and 3d, the IC50 value for P-CDs against ABTS radicals was 2.48 μg/mL, slightly lower than vitamin C's 3.21 μg/mL, indicating that P-CDs have a greater advantage in scavenging ABTS radicals.

In summary, this study demonstrated the superior performance of P-CDs in scavenging DPPH and ABTS radicals, as shown through quantitative analysis and experimental observation. These findings offer valuable insights for further research into the use of P-CDs in antioxidation.

When frying at high temperatures, triglycerides undergo hydrolysis and thermo-oxidative degradation, which converts them into free fatty acids (FFAs). This process leads to a significant increase in the acid value (AV) of frying oils. As a result, AV can serve as a reliable indicator of FFA content, reflecting the extent of oxidative rancidity in oil. Fig. 4 a illustrates the impact of various antioxidants on the AV of frying oil. During the frying process, AV increased substantially in all oil samples. The control sample had the most significant increase, going from 0 mg KOH/g before frying to 2.23 mg KOH/g after frying, which is higher than in the antioxidant-treated samples. In comparison, oil samples with butylhydroxyanisole (BHA) had a final AV of 0.65 mg KOH/g. Notably, oil samples treated with P-CDs showed the slowest increase in AV, with a final value of 0.35 mg KOH/g, significantly lower than other treatment groups. The ranking order for retarding the increase in AV among all antioxidants is: 0.01% P-CDs >0.01% PG > 0.01% BHA.

Peroxides are key products of lipid oxidation and maintain a dynamic balance between their formation and breakdown during frying (Blasi et al., 2018). At the start of deep-frying, the rate of peroxide formation exceeds the rate of its breakdown, leading to an increase in the peroxide value (PV). As shown in Fig. 4b, the PV of all sample groups gradually increased with longer frying times. After 5 h of frying, oil samples with added PG had the lowest PV, measuring 5.42 mmol/kg. This was similar to the PV of samples with added P-CDs, which was 5.43 mmol/kg. It's worth noting that the PV values for the 0.01% PG and 0.01% P-CDs treatment groups did not significantly differ after 5 h of frying. The impacts of the different antioxidants on slowing the increase in PV can be ranked as follows: 0.01% PG > 0.01% P-CDs >0.01% BHA.

The p-anisidine value (p-AnV) measures the content of aldehydes formed during oil degradation and is a key indicator of oil oxidation. Because p-AnV does not easily break down at high temperatures, it is a crucial parameter for evaluating oil oxidation levels (Heng et al., 2020). Compared to the peroxide value (PV), p-AnV is often more accurate and sensitive in indicating the extent of oil oxidation. As shown in Fig. 4c, there were significant changes in the p-AnV of the control oil sample during the heating process. Initially, the p-AnV was 0, but after 5 h of heating, it increased dramatically to 128.08, indicating substantial oxidative degradation of oils and fats during this period. To reduce the increase in p-AnV and limit oxidative degradation, adding antioxidants is an effective strategy. In this study, P-CDs had the strongest delaying effect, with a p-AnV of 60.27 after 5 h of heating, significantly outperforming BHA and PG. The order of effectiveness for slowing the increase in p-AnV was: 0.01% P-CDs >0.01% BHA >0.01% PG. This finding provides a new approach for controlling the quality of fat preservation and fried foods.

Based on these results, 0.01% P-CDs demonstrated an outstanding ability to slow the rise in AV, while there was no significant difference in PV between 0.01% PG and 0.01% P-CDs; both performed exceptionally well. Additionally, adding antioxidants proved effective in delaying the increase in p-AnV. P-CDs showed the most substantial oxidation-delaying effect, outperforming BHA and PG.

Thermal oxidation of oil during heating can be assessed by examining changes in fatty acid composition (Fig. 5 ). Some minor fatty acids, likely due to their low content (<3%), showed no significant changes during heating and are not included in Fig. 5. However, the major fatty acids experienced notable changes after 5 h of heating. Specifically, there was a reduction in linolenic (C18:3) and linoleic (C18:2) acids, while oleic (C18:1), stearic (C18:0), and palmitic (C16:0) acids increased. This shift occurs because double bonds in some unsaturated fatty acids are broken during frying, leading to their conversion into saturated fatty acids (SFAs), thereby increasing the SFA content. The most significant increases in C18:1, C18:0, and C16:0 was seen in the control oil samples without added antioxidants, while the smallest increases were in oils with added P-CDs, followed by those with PG. The smallest reduction in C18:2 and C18:3 was observed in oils containing BHA, with slightly larger decreases in those containing PG.

During the frying process, oxidation byproducts like ketones, hydroperoxides, hydroxides, and conjugated dienoic acids build up, gradually changing the color of the oils from a light yellow to a dark brown. This change not only impacts the appearance of the oils but also reflects the transformation of their internal properties (Sun et al., 2019).

Based on CIELAB color systems, as indicated in Table 1 , experimental measurements showed a gradual decline in both the L* and b* values across all samples as frying time increased. This suggests that the brightness and yellow tone of the oils gradually decreased during frying. However, the a* value, which measures the red hue, showed an upward trend for all samples except those with P-CDs. This increase may be due to the contribution of certain oxidation byproducts to the red hue. Notably, the a* value for the P-CDs sample remained relatively stable, around 2.15, which may be linked to its unique antioxidant mechanism.

A further comparison of the data from various samples shows that the L* and b* values of the control sample were significantly lower than those of the samples treated with antioxidants. This finding underscores the effectiveness of antioxidants in reducing color changes in oils during frying. Notably, P-CDs not only slow down color changes but their own reddish hue might help mask the change in oil chromaticity, making the overall color shift less pronounced.

This trend was confirmed through visual observation. As the frying time increased, the color of all oil samples, except those with added P-CDs, darkened significantly. However, the color change in the P-CDs oil samples was relatively minor, demonstrating their superior ability to maintain oil color.

In summary, antioxidants play a crucial role in preserving the color of oils during frying, with P-CDs showing exceptional potential in preventing color changes due to their unique properties. These insights are valuable for optimizing the frying process, enhancing food quality, and extending the lifespan of cooking oils.

In the long-term application of frying oil, its viscosity change is an important index to measure the stability and shelf-life of oil. Studies have confirmed that the quality, type and operating temperature of oil have a significant effect on the viscosity of frying oil (Sivaranjani et al., 2024). With the extension of frying time, the oil undergoes complex physical and chemical changes at high temperature, in which the gradual formation of macromolecular substances is the key factor leading to the increase of viscosity (Sharoba & Hassanien, 2012). These macromolecules may be derived from the oxidation, polymerization or thermal cracking of oils and fats, and their formation increases the degree of polymerization of oils, thereby increasing their viscosity.

The experimental results shown in Fig. 6 reveal the significant role of antioxidants in inhibiting the increase of frying oil viscosity (p < 0.05). Compared with the control samples without antioxidants, the samples added with BHA, PG and P-CDs had a significantly smaller increase in viscosity after heating for 5 h. This finding indicates that antioxidants can effectively inhibit the oxidation reaction in oil and reduce the formation of macromolecular substances, thereby slowing the increase in the viscosity of frying oil.

P-CDs show unique advantages when comparing the effects of different antioxidants. As a new type of antioxidant, P-CDs can not only effectively inhibit the oxidation reaction of oil, but also have the smallest increase in viscosity during heating. This finding indicates that P-CDs have excellent antioxidant properties and stability, and is expected to become a substitute for traditional antioxidants such as BHA and PG.

Carbon dots (P-CDs), derived from red wine polyphenols, were synthesized, characterized, and then used to enhance the oxidative stability of sunflower oil. The resulting P-CDs were spherical nanoparticles with good monodispersity, generally larger than 0.5 nm. This unique structure is attributed to oxygen-containing functional groups (O–H, CO, and C–O), identified through FTIR spectroscopy at wavelengths of 3400, 1705, and 1035 cm−1, respectively. The ability of P-CDs to neutralize DPPH and ABTS radicals represents a significant advance in the field of antioxidants. Both of these radicals can cause cellular damage, leading to various health issues. P-CDs' scavenging of these radicals demonstrates their strong antioxidant potential. Moreover, P-CDs also performed remarkably well in preventing the oxidative rancidity of oils, which typically occurs during high-temperature heating and generates compounds harmful to human health. From the key indicators of AV, PV, and p-AnV in oil samples, the effectiveness of P-CDs is on par with, or in some cases better than, leading antioxidants available in the market. P-CDs effectively reduced AV, PV, and p-AnV while maintaining the lipid acid composition, color, and viscosity of the oils. Thus, these carbon dots have the potential to replace synthetic antioxidants and other natural antioxidants in stabilizing oils used in high-heat applications. This breakthrough not only sheds light on P-CDs' effectiveness in reducing oxidative rancidity in frying oils but also paves the way for innovative approaches to oil preservation, contributing to the growth of the food industry.

This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 32060534 and 32360571), Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (Natural Science) (Grant No. ZK [2023] 118, 166), Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology Support Project (Grant No. [2023] 343), Guizhou Provincial Department of Information Industry Project (Grant No. [2020]198), Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Guizhou Province (Qianke Zhongyindi [2019]4019), General horizontal project (Grant No. K22-0115-014, Qianrenlingfa [2020]8), Guizhou University Natural Science Special Scientific Research Fund Project (X2021327), Talents Introduction Projects of Guizhou University (Grant No. [2020]5) and Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (Natural Science) (Grant No. ZK [2024] Major Project 016).

Minxian Duan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Guohuan Huang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yuanlin Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Data curation. Yongguang Huang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Zaichun Xu from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM analysis.
